title: Learning a Distribution, Not Just a Label
concept: The core idea behind generative modeling: instead of learning a boundary between categories, a generative model learns the underlying distribution of the training data — the full shape and spread of what "plausible" examples look like — well enough to sample brand-new points from it.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is one way generative models can help with an imbalanced dataset, as an alternative to weighted loss (Unit 128)?

options.text: By synthesizing brand-new, plausible examples of the rare class
By deleting extra examples from the majority class
By relabeling majority-class examples as minority-class

exercises.explain: Yes! Generating new minority-class examples directly addresses data scarcity, rather than just reweighting the loss.

cards.title: Digit one thousand and one
cards.body: Hand someone a thousand handwritten digits and ask them to write digit number one thousand and one — one that still looks like a real handwritten digit, but isn't a copy of any single one they saw. To pull that off, they need to understand the underlying shape rules of digits, not just memorize a list of a thousand images.

cards.title: What is a distribution?
cards.body: A distribution describes the full range and likelihood of all the values a variable can take. A generative model learns the training data's distribution — which pixel arrangements are "plausible images" versus meaningless noise — well enough to sample brand-new, plausible points from that learned space.

cards.title: Learning the recipe, not one cookie
cards.body: A chef who tastes a thousand chocolate-chip cookies learns the underlying recipe pattern — the ratios of sugar to flour, the right chewiness, how many chips per bite. That chef can now bake a brand-new cookie that fits the pattern. A chef who only memorized one exact cookie can just reproduce that single cookie, nothing more.

cards.title: Sampling a new face
cards.body: A generative model trained on thousands of face photos learns the distribution of "plausible face" pixel arrangements — two roughly symmetric eyes, a centered nose, skin-tone color ranges that occur together. To create a new face, it samples a fresh point from that learned space, producing a face that fits the pattern but was never a specific person.

cards.title: It's not a giant lookup table
cards.body: It's easy to assume the model secretly stores a giant table of every training image to remix. In reality, training compresses the entire pattern of the dataset into a comparatively small set of internal parameters — a compact mathematical description of "what plausible data looks like," not a copy of any file.

cards.title: Boundary vs. distribution
cards.body: Two very different things to learn from the same data:

left.text:
Learns a boundary between classes
Only needs to know which side
an example falls on

right.text:
Learns the full shape of the data
Can sample brand-new points
from anywhere inside that shape

exercises.prompt: What does a generative model learn about its training data?

options.text: The underlying distribution — the range and pattern of plausible examples
A single boundary line that separates two categories
A fixed list of every training file, stored for playback
Nothing; it only needs labels to function

exercises.explain: Correct! Learning the distribution is what lets a generative model sample brand-new, plausible examples.

exercises.prompt: True or false: To generate a new example, a generative model samples a fresh point from the distribution it learned during training.
exercises.explain: True. Sampling from the learned distribution is exactly how new, plausible examples get produced.

exercises.sentence: A generative model learns the underlying ___ of the training data so it can sample new, plausible examples.
exercises.explain: Learning the distribution — not just a boundary or a label — is what enables sampling brand-new examples.

exercises.prompt: Which behavior shows a model has learned a distribution rather than just a decision boundary?

options.text: It can produce a brand-new, plausible example that fits the training pattern but matches no single training file
It can sort a new example into "Class A" or "Class B" with high accuracy
It can draw a tight box around an object in a photo

exercises.explain: Correct! Producing new plausible examples is the signature capability of learning a distribution.

exercises.prompt: Two models are trained on 10,000 photos of birds. Model A draws a boundary that separates "bird" from "not bird." Model B learns the overall pattern of bird shapes, colors, and feather textures well enough to sample brand-new bird images. Which model has learned a distribution, and what capability does that unlock?

options.text: Model B — learning the distribution lets it generate brand-new, plausible bird images that were never in the training set
Model A — drawing a boundary always implies the model also learned the full distribution
Both models learned the same thing, since both were trained on the same 10,000 photos
Neither model learned a distribution, since distributions only apply to numeric data

exercises.explain: Exactly! Model B learned the shape of the data, so it can sample new points from within it. Next: could a model you already know — the autoencoder — do this too?
